title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption:
The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.

Three lines trace the female-to-male wage ratio across the United States from 1980 to 2010, with the vertical axis measuring the percentage of male wages earned by women and the horizontal axis marking time in five-year increments. The chart distinguishes three income groups: the tenth percentile, the fiftieth percentile, and the ninetieth percentile. In 1980, all three lines begin below the 70% mark, with the tenth percentile starting highest at roughly 65%, followed by the ninetieth percentile near 62%, and the fiftieth percentile lowest at approximately 60%. Over the subsequent three decades, the lines for the tenth and fiftieth percentiles show a consistent upward trajectory. By 2010, both the tenth and fiftieth percentile lines converge near the 82% level, indicating that women in the lower and middle income brackets closed a significant portion of the wage gap relative to men in those same brackets. The line for the ninetieth percentile follows a different path; it rises from 1980 to roughly 1990, reaching a peak near 75%, before leveling off and remaining flat through 2010. This creates a visible divergence where the highest earners among women saw their wage ratio stagnate in the later years of the period, while the ratios for the tenth and fiftieth percentiles continued to climb until they surpassed the ninetieth percentile line around 1998. The data shows that while wage ratios improved across all measured income levels between 1980 and 2010, the rate of improvement varied by income group, resulting in a convergence of the lower and middle percentiles and a plateau for the highest percentile.
